task: I’m getting my tires rotated soon and want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything basic. Can you help me figure out what I should check before I drive off, and whether there’s anything specific I should ask them to verify while it’s on the lift?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple **before-you-leave tire rotation checklist** plus a few **lift-specific things to ask them to verify**.

## Before you drive off
Do a quick walkaround and confirm:

- **Lug nuts are present and seated** on every wheel
- **Valve stems/caps** are intact
- **Tires are on the correct corners** if they’re doing a rotation pattern you requested
- **No fresh scuffs, gouges, or wheel damage**
- **No tire pressure warning light**
- **Tire pressures are set correctly** for your car
- **Wheel covers/hubcaps** are fully clipped on, if applicable
- **No unusual vibration or pulling** on the short drive out

If you can, check the **torque spec on the invoice** or ask whether they **torqued the lugs with a torque wrench** after install.

## While it’s on the lift, ask them to verify:
These are the basics worth checking while the wheels are off:

- **Brake pad thickness**
- **Brake rotor condition**  
  - deep grooves
  - cracks
  - heavy lip on the edge
- **Tire wear pattern**
  - uneven inner/outer wear
  - cupping/scalloping
  - feathering
- **Suspension play**
  - tie rods
  - ball joints
  - control arm bushings
  - wheel bearings
- **Leaks**
  - around struts/shocks
  - axle seals
  - brake lines
- **Tire age / damage**
  - sidewall cracks
  - bulges
  - punctures or embedded debris

## Good questions to ask
- “Can you show me if any tire wear looks abnormal?”
- “Do the brakes have plenty of life left?”
- “Any play in the bearings or steering/suspension parts?”
- “Did you torque the lugs to spec?”
- “Any leaks or damaged boots/seals you noticed?”

## One thing to do after you leave
After **25–50 miles**, recheck:
- tire pressure
- TPMS light
- any new vibration, noise, or pulling

If you want, I can turn this into a **1-minute printable checklist** you can keep in your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid, but I’d want it a little tighter and more specific to the lift work. The basic walkaround is fine.

What I’d add:
- Ask them to **mark which tire came off which corner** if they’re rotating by position. I like knowing the pattern actually got followed.
- Have them check the **spare and torque wrench calibration** only if they used a shop torque wrench — not everyone does, but if they say they torqued to spec, I want the actual spec number.
- Ask them to look at **inner edge wear** while the tires are off. That’s the stuff you miss if you only glance at the outside tread.
- If they see uneven wear, ask whether it looks like **alignment, inflation, or suspension**. Don’t let them hand-wave it as “normal.”
- On the lift, I’d also ask them to check **CV boots** and **strut mounts** if the car has them. Cheap to spot when the wheel’s hanging, expensive later if ignored.

And before you leave, I’d personally want:
- lug torque confirmed
- tire pressures written down or at least verified
- no TPMS light
- no new vibration on the first few miles

I’ve had a shop once install a tire wrong on the pressure rating, so yeah, I’m pretty annoying about specs now. One small miss turns into a second trip, and I’d rather catch it before I pull out of the lot.

If you want, I can give you a short version that’s basically just the exact questions to say at the counter.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — here’s a tighter version you can actually use.

## Before you leave
Do the quick walkaround, but focus on these:

- **TPMS light off**
- **Tire pressures verified** and set to the door-jamb spec
- **Lug torque confirmed**
- **No wheel damage, scuffs, or missing caps**
- **No vibration/pull on the first short drive**

If they rotated by position, I’d also want to know **which tire came off which corner** so you can confirm the pattern was actually followed.

## While it’s on the lift, ask them to check
These are the useful lift-side checks:

- **Inner edge tread wear**
- **Outer edge tread wear**
- **Uneven wear pattern**
  - alignment
  - inflation
  - suspension
- **CV boots** for cracks/grease sling
- **Strut mounts**
- **Brake pads and rotors**
- **Tie rods / ball joints / wheel bearing play**
- **Any leaks** around shocks, axles, or brakes

If they see uneven wear, ask directly:
- “Does this look more like **alignment, inflation, or suspension**?”

That keeps them from brushing it off as normal.

## Specific things to ask
You can say:

- “Which tire came off which corner?”
- “What’s the **lug torque spec** you used?”
- “Can you check the **inner tread edges** while the wheels are off?”
- “Do the **CV boots and strut mounts** look okay?”
- “If there’s uneven wear, what’s the most likely cause?”

## One thing I’d add
If they touched the wheels, I’d want:
- **torque confirmed**
- **pressures written down or verified**
- **no TPMS light**
- **no new noise/vibration after a few miles**

If you want, I can turn this into a very short **counter script** — basically the exact 4–5 questions to ask without sounding over the top.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically the version I wanted. Tight enough, and the lift checks are the part that matters.

The only thing I’d maybe keep in mind is to ask whether they actually **checked the inner edges with the wheel hanging**, not just a glance from the ground. That’s where the useful wear pattern shows up.

Otherwise this is good. The counter script would probably be the most useful next step.
